Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Right Fast, averaging 132.3 kph (up to 138.7).
  • Typical length is 7-8m (their good length); median 7.0 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length outside off, swinging both ways, seaming away, hitting the top of off (32% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 1% of the time against right-handers.
  • Across an over he varies his length a lot.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Full (7 wkts, 3.4% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: 5th stump.
  • Takes 21% of wickets pitching a good length wide outside off.
  • Beats the bat 8.7% of tracked balls (false-shot 20.1%).
  • Gets you bowled more than most — 29% of his wickets (1.5× the typical rate for this type).
  • 94% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, slip: 1st, slip: 4th.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length on the 6th stump (20% of runs).
  • Short ball: 28% of deliveries, economy 3.7 — 5 wkts from 362 short balls.
  • Away from full, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak square/through the off side (47%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes full outside off (economy 4.3, beats the bat only 18%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length outside off, swinging away, reverses in, seaming away, hitting the top of off (32% of deliveries, economy 2.90). Minor variations: a good length wide outside off (25%) and full outside off (14%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length outside off ▶swinging away, reverses in, seaming away32%2.90921%
a good length wide outside off ▶swinging away, reverses in, seaming away25%2.67620%
full outside off ▶swinging away, reverses in, seaming both ways14%4.33418%
full wide outside off ▶swinging away, reverses in, seaming away5%8.00512%
back of a length wide outside off ▶seaming both ways, swinging away, reverses in5%4.68034%
a good length in the channel ▶swinging away, reverses in, seaming away4%2.81226%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

55% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 12 balls; most runs go to the off side (47%); the drive hurts most (50 fours/sixes at 2.6 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps — pitches around wide of 6th and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the off side (51%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length wide outside off most often (25%); drops short often (28%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length wide outside off, but strikes most often pitching on the 5th stump.

Speed & Spells

He is ~2.4 km/h quicker in later spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and loses ~1.7 km/h by the later days.

By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.
Speed by Day
By match day — fatigue across the game.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Variable with his length (length spread ±2.5 m; more repeatable than 64% of pace bowlers); with no consistent trend to his length across the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.
Across 31 dismissals, the wicket ball lands about 43 cm fuller than the ball before it — that's the change that brings the wicket.
Ball in overMedian lengthShort %EconWkt %

Ball 37.0 m6%2.962.8%
Ball 47.0 m8%3.201.4%
Ball 57.0 m7%3.803.3%
Ball 66.9 m5%4.701.9%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 86% of right-arm pace); releases about 68 cm from the middle stump; varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 96% of right-arm pace). Comes wider round the wicket (68 cm over vs 88 cm round).

Movement (vs the average pace bowler · vs right-handers)

A swing bowler who swings it away with the new ball (64%) and reverses it back in when it's old (59%).
Generates below avg seam and above avg swing for a pace bowler; seams it away more to right-hand batters (65%).
Swing by ball age to right-hand batters: new ball (≤25 ov, n=352) 64% away / 36% in; old ball (≥40 ov, n=322) 41% away / 59% in — the swing direction reverses with the old ball.
Percentile vs all Test pace bowlers; direction is which way it moves to this batter. Bounce = extra bounce vs expected.

Beats the bat most at Good Length / Wide of 6th (31% of play-and-misses). Overall he beats the bat 8.7% of tracked balls (false-shot 20.1%, n=1,290).
Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
